timeline.current_model: GPX4 is a selenocysteine-containing lipid hydroperoxidase that reduces phospholipid hydroperoxides to non-toxic lipid alcohols using glutathione as the reducing substrate, thereby preventing iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and ferroptosis; its activity, stability, and subcellular localization are regulated at multiple levels including selenocysteine-based catalysis (superior to Cys for resisting overoxidation), alternative promoter-driven isoform expression (cytosolic, mitochondrial, nuclear), and an extensive post-translational modification network encompassing K48/K63/M1-linked ubiquitination by E3 ligases (STUB1, NEDD4L, CUL1-FBW7) and stabilization by deubiquitinases (USP8, OTUD5, OTUB1), S-palmitoylation at C66/C75 by ZDHHC20/ZDHHC8 (reversed by APT2), arginine-152 methylation by PRMT5, and serine-104 phosphorylation by CKB; direct covalent modification at C66 by itaconate allosterically activates GPX4; copper-induced ubiquitination at C107/C148 and chaperone-mediated autophagy (legumain/HSC70) or selective autophagy (TAX1BP1, TRAF6-p62) drive its degradation; transcriptional control by STAT3, KLF11, and epigenetic mechanisms (m6A via METTL3/ALKBH5/FTO, DNMT-mediated promoter methylation) modulates GPX4 levels; functionally, GPX4 is essential for TFH cell survival, innate immune STING activation (by preventing lipid peroxidation-induced STING carbonylation at C88), and host resistance to Mycobacterium tuberculosis, and in adipocytes it suppresses metabolic inflammation independently of overt ferroptosis.
